runs.text: 1. Coverage
Measure the unbucketed share; extend the entity registry until ~90% of dollars are classified; bring DDG pulls to parity with the submarine corpus.

text_body.plain_text:
1. Coverage
Measure the unbucketed share; extend the entity registry until ~90% of dollars are classified; bring DDG pulls to parity with the submarine corpus.

runs.text: 2. Score
Compute pool size, concentration, entry, and cadence per work type from the existing FY22–25 subaward corpus.

text_body.plain_text:
2. Score
Compute pool size, concentration, entry, and cadence per work type from the existing FY22–25 subaward corpus.

runs.text: 3. Overlay
Assign barrier height per work type and attach seeding evidence from industrial-base and prime sourcing announcements.

text_body.plain_text:
3. Overlay
Assign barrier height per work type and attach seeding evidence from industrial-base and prime sourcing announcements.

runs.text: 4. Target
Rank vendors within the highest-scoring work types; each vendor’s award history yields the named competable awards.

text_body.plain_text:
4. Target
Rank vendors within the highest-scoring work types; each vendor’s award history yields the named competable awards.

runs.text: Methodology

text_body.plain_text: Methodology

runs.text: Unit of analysis: the seven existing work types.

Competability is reported per work type — the same mutually exclusive structural / machining / castings / piping / electrical / HVAC / coatings vocabulary already used for the pool sizing — so the competable pool nests inside exhibits the audience has already seen. All signals are computed per vendor and rolled up: the ranked vendor list inside each work type, with each vendor’s award history, is itself the specific-award target list. No new taxonomy is introduced.
Why structure, not narrative: subaward descriptions are unusable.

Prime filings carry boilerplate description text, so work content cannot be read from the award records. Classification therefore rides on the vendor — entity registry, NAICS, and name evidence — and competability rides on observable market structure: who shares the work, who is entering, how often the buy recurs.
Demand-side overlay: where second sources are being actively seeded.

Quantitative structure is crossed with stated intent — submarine industrial-base supplier-development announcements, prime supplier-day solicitations, and the PB27 Strategic Outsourcing narrative — to separate “fragmented because competed” from “single-source and the customer wants out.”

text_body.plain_text:
Unit of analysis: the seven existing work types. Competability is reported per work type — the same mutually exclusive structural / machining / castings / piping / electrical / HVAC / coatings vocabulary already used for the pool sizing — so the competable pool nests inside exhibits the audience has already seen. All signals are computed per vendor and rolled up: the ranked vendor list inside each work type, with each vendor’s award history, is itself the specific-award target list. No new taxonomy is introduced.
Why structure, not narrative: subaward descriptions are unusable. Prime filings carry boilerplate description text, so work content cannot be read from the award records. Classification therefore rides on the vendor — entity registry, NAICS, and name evidence — and competability rides on observable market structure: who shares the work, who is entering, how often the buy recurs.
Demand-side overlay: where second sources are being actively seeded. Quantitative structure is crossed with stated intent — submarine industrial-base supplier-development announcements, prime supplier-day solicitations, and the PB27 Strategic Outsourcing narrative — to separate “fragmented because competed” from “single-source and the customer wants out.”

runs.text: Excluded from the competable pool: the ~$4.2B BlueForge Alliance subaward flow — supplier-development and workforce funding routed through the Columbia prime contract, not component purchases — and Navy-directed GFE primes, where the competition is run by the Navy, not the shipbuilder.
First-tier subaward reporting is incomplete, so work-type pool sizes are floors; concentration, entry, and cadence survive undercounting better than levels do.

text_body.plain_text:
Excluded from the competable pool: the ~$4.2B BlueForge Alliance subaward flow — supplier-development and workforce funding routed through the Columbia prime contract, not component purchases — and Navy-directed GFE primes, where the competition is run by the Navy, not the shipbuilder.
First-tier subaward reporting is incomplete, so work-type pool sizes are floors; concentration, entry, and cadence survive undercounting better than levels do.

runs.text: Sources: FSRS / SAM.gov first-tier subaward reporting, FY22–25 full history, prime shipbuilding contract PIIDs for Virginia, Columbia, and DDG-51; FPDS-NG award records; SAM entity registrations; PB27 P-10 Strategic Outsourcing narrative; submarine industrial-base supplier-development announcements and prime supplier-opportunity solicitations
